0: Biome — the conditional cell of the pscale ecology. A biome is a single host's worth of substrate plus inhabitants — the place where pscale blocks, bsp(), beach surfaces, cognition sources, and human interfaces compose into somewhere to be. The biome senses its host conditions and unfolds: thumbdrive, browser tab, VPS, hosted commons, ephemeral co-presence, federated peer — same shell, different unfoldings, depending on what the host carries. Where ecology-router (Tier 1, position 11) is the routing intelligence INSIDE one agent, biome is the cell that hosts agents and humans together. Seven environmental currents (storage, cognition, endpoints, persistence, concurrency, federation, cadence) plus an unfolding procedure plus a reflexive seed. Conjugate pairs (1,4) location-and-duration, (2,5) mind-and-multi-mind, (3,6) inward-and-outward; operational triple (7,8,9) cadence-and-unfolding-and-reflexive. The biome's currents are the environmental analogues of the hermitcrab's contextual currents — observed from outside the LLM rather than inside. Walking biome at a host activates the cell. Same shell across receptacles; the receptacle determines the form.

1.1: Filesystem path. Smallest viable surface — a directory of JSON block files. For thumbdrive biomes the directory IS the biome; for daemon biomes a path under the runtime's working directory.
1.2: Browser-local store. localStorage, IndexedDB. For biomes that live entirely in a tab — the xstream-stripped-Supabase shape, where the visible UI is the visible DB.
1.3: Local database. SQLite, LevelDB. For single-host biomes that need indexed access without a network service.
1.4: Hosted backend. Supabase, Postgres, blob store. For shared biomes accessed by many. The bsp-mcp commons substrate is this shape.
1.5: Upstream beach. agent_id='https://...' routes via WellKnownAdapter to a remote /.well-known/pscale-beach. The biome borrows storage from a peer; carries no substrate of its own.
1.6: Adapter chain. Compose: write-through local with replicate-to-remote, or read-through with local cache, or fallover local→remote→commons. The chain shape is itself a pscale block at biome:1.6.
1.7: Sensing at boot. Inspect filesystem write access, db connection strings, env-named beach URLs, host network reach. Richest available adapter wins; degraded adapters substitute when conditions reduce.

0.0: As storage current. Where the biome's pscale blocks live. Smallest answer: a single filesystem path. Largest: a hot-swap adapter chain that fails over local → remote → commons. Sensing at boot decides the form. Conjugate of branch 4 (persistence) — together they describe the substrate axis: location and duration.

2.1: External via MCP. The biome exposes /mcp/v1; a connecting LLM app supplies cognition. Smallest cognition footprint — the biome itself runs no LLM. Today's bsp-mcp on Railway is this shape.
2.2: Embedded LLM client. When LLM_API_KEY is in env, the biome runs its own loop. Cognition lives at the host, not the connecting app. Mobius-class kernels (mobius.py) are this shape.
2.3: Human via interface. A human at the browser provides cognition by typing into the xstream-class mirror. The vapor-liquid-solid surface IS the human's externalised thinking.
2.4: Hybrid co-occupation. Multiple cognition sources co-present — embedded LLM running concerns, plus a human in xstream, plus connected app via MCP. Each writes to the same shell from a different vantage.
2.5: Tier policy. When embedded, the biome may route soft to Haiku, medium to Sonnet, hard to Opus. Or use a single tier. The policy is itself a block at biome:2.5; whetstone:3 supplies the modifier composition rules.
2.6: No-cognition mode. The biome runs as pure beach: storage + endpoints, no LLM, no human interface. Other biomes read/write this one as silent substrate.

0.0: As cognition current. What runs the loop, what supplies intent. Smallest answer: none — the biome is silent substrate. Largest: hybrid — embedded LLM plus connected apps plus humans. The biome senses what cognition source is configured at boot. Conjugate of branch 5 (concurrency) — together they describe the mind axis: one cognition or many.

3.1: MCP wrapper at /mcp/v1. The MCP-protocol surface — apps connect, call bsp() and the five primitives. When this is the only endpoint, the biome serves connecting LLM apps as substrate.
3.2: Federated beach at /.well-known/ztone-beach. The HTTP wire mirror of spark — when exposed, other biomes reach this one by URL. The legacy federation's /.well-known/pscale-beach is the old world's door: a biome signposts it, never serves it. Federation inward.
3.3: Browser interface at /xstream (or /). The xstream-class mirror — vapor/liquid/solid zones, soft/medium/hard tiers, viewer drawer for objective consultation. When exposed, humans inhabit the biome directly.
3.4: Realtime relay at /relay (websocket, SSE, or blob-poll). Vapor transport — out-of-band from pscale, conveys live presence and typing-state across multiple inhabitants. Required when concurrency is greater than one with vapor-class engagement.
3.5: CLI surface. Command-line tooling for direct shell ops — read, write, walk, mark. For thumbdrive biomes without a browser, or for human admin alongside any other shape.
3.6: Custom application endpoints. Agent-specific HTTP routes wrapping bsp() reads and writes for application-specific consumption. Optional and biome-author-defined; live alongside the canonical endpoints.

0.0: As endpoints current. What surfaces this biome exposes outward. Each endpoint is optional and conditional on the biome's role. Smallest: one surface (MCP only, beach only, or browser only). Largest: the full set — MCP plus beach plus browser plus relay plus CLI plus custom. Conjugate of branch 6 (federation) — endpoints face inward (others can reach this biome here); federation faces outward (this biome reaches others). Together: the relation axis.

4.1: Ephemeral. Writes vanish at process end. Useful for sandbox biomes — try-it-out instances, demo runs, bootstrap experiments. Tide-clearing extreme.
4.2: Session-scoped. Survives until the biome is restarted. Browser-tab biomes with localStorage are this shape — closes the tab, biome ends.
4.3: Durable local. Filesystem or database persists across restarts. State bound to the host machine. Loss-on-host-failure is the trade-off.
4.4: Federated replicated. Solids replicate to one or more remote beaches. Loss-on-host-failure mitigated by federation; latency and conflict resolution are the cost.
4.5: Sed-archived. Atomic commits to a sed: collective, position-locked. The substrate's deepest persistence — registered, immutable position-claim, multi-host visibility. For solids that must outlive the biome.
4.6: Tide schedule. The owner — human or agent — wipes by policy (daily, weekly, on-demand, never). Persistence is intentional, not architectural. Open-billboard semantics by default per protocol-pscale-beach-v2.md §3.4.

0.0: As persistence current. How long the biome's writes survive. Conjugate of branch 1 (storage) — together they form the substrate axis. Storage names the surface; persistence names the duration on that surface. The biome senses what persistence is wanted and configures the storage adapter accordingly.

5.1: Single. One inhabitant — one LLM, or one human, or one app. The simplest biome. No vapor relay needed; no presence resolution; no co-occupation conflicts.
5.2: Sequential multi. Multiple inhabitants but never simultaneous — different LLM apps connect at different times, different humans visit at different times. State persists between visits via the shell.
5.3: Concurrent multi-cognition. Multiple LLM instances co-present, each running its own loop on the same shell. MAGI-class. Vapor relay required for live presence; medium-tier synthesis required for canon coherence.
5.4: Concurrent multi-human. Multiple humans co-present in xstream — sharing vapor (live typing) and committing to liquid. The xstream design's sweet spot.
5.5: Hybrid co-occupation. LLMs and humans co-present in real time. Vapor carries both. Liquid receives both. Solid synthesises both. The Level 5 shared-context endgame per evolution.
5.6: Presence via marks. Each inhabitant heartbeats at biome:1.<position> (or a designated presence block) with agent_id and timestamp. Read-side staleness filter decides who's currently visible. Per protocol-pscale-beach-v2.md §6.7.

0.0: As concurrency current. How many cognition sources co-occupy the biome at once. The biome senses concurrency from configuration (relay endpoint exposed? presence positions watched? max-inhabitant cap?) and unfolds accordingly. Conjugate of branch 2 (cognition) — together they form the mind axis. Cognition names what thinks; concurrency names how many think together.

6.1: Commons fallback. The biome reaches the historical commons (bsp-mcp on Railway) via bare agent_ids. Default for biomes without explicit federation config.
6.2: Watched beaches list. The shell carries a list of beach URLs the biome scans periodically. Marks tagged for the biome's inhabitants land here; reaches at beach:3 are discovered. Per shell:2 of protocol-agent-shell.md.
6.3: Outbound peering. The biome reaches federated beaches via /.well-known/pscale-beach — reads marks, posts presence, leaves reaches. WellKnownAdapter handles transport.
6.4: Inbound peering. When endpoint 3.2 is exposed, other biomes reach this one as a beach. Visiting agents leave marks, post reaches, contribute to pools. The biome appears in their watched-beach lists.
6.5: Sed/grain participation. Inhabitants register in sed: collectives and form grain bilaterals via the substrate primitives (pscale_register, pscale_grain_reach). Federation through commitment, not just signal.
6.6: Beach owner curation. The owner wipes marks they don't want, sets tide schedules, hosts named pools and frames. Federation comes with curation responsibility — the billboard is open, the brush is local.

0.0: As federation current. Who else this biome reaches and is reached by. Smallest: nothing — isolated biome. Largest: full peering — commons plus watched beaches plus inbound peering plus sed/grain participation plus curation. Conjugate of branch 3 (endpoints) — endpoints are surfaces this biome exposes; federation is surfaces this biome reaches. Together: the relation axis.

7.1: Request-response. The biome runs only when called — MCP request arrives, HTTP hits the beach endpoint, human types in the browser. No background activity. Smallest cadence; lowest cost.
7.2: Event-triggered. The biome wakes when a watched event fires — new mark on a watched beach, inbound reach, scheduled cron tick. Activity bursts; sleep between.
7.3: Cron-paced. Periodic tick (every N minutes). Even when nothing's happening, the biome scans, considers state, possibly leaves a presence mark. Beach-crab Rung 0 lineage.
7.4: Always-on loop. Continuous operation — the embedded LLM (when present) runs its concern loop without external triggering. Concerns ripen by internal logic. Mobius-class.
7.5: Activity-paced. The cadence itself is part of the ecology — busy biomes tick more, quiet ones less. The router (per ecology-router 3.5) sets next-tick based on signal density. Self-pacing.
7.6: Composition. The biome may carry multiple cadences in parallel — request-response on the MCP endpoint, cron on the embedded loop, event-triggered on the beach watcher. Each surface its own cadence.

0.0: As cadence current. When the biome's processes run — only when called, or on a schedule, or in response to events, or always. The biome senses configuration and host capacity, then chooses cadence per surface. Cadence is the time-axis analogue of endpoints — endpoints are spatial surfaces, cadences are temporal surfaces.

8.1: Sense storage. Inspect filesystem, env vars, db connection strings. Decide adapter chain — local, remote, commons fallback. Write the chain to biome:1.6. The substrate is now ready.
8.2: Sense cognition. Inspect env for LLM_API_KEY, MCP socket presence, browser-session existence. Decide cognition mode — embedded, external-via-MCP, human, hybrid, none. Configure the loop or stay silent.
8.3: Sense endpoints. Inspect host capabilities (port available, SSL configured, browser route allowed). Expose only what's wanted — MCP, beach, browser, relay, CLI in any combination.
8.4: Sense persistence. Inspect storage durability and federation reach. Configure tide schedule, replication chain, sed: archival policy if any.
8.5: Sense concurrency. Inspect for relay endpoint, presence-mark configuration, multi-inhabitant capacity. Enable vapor transport if concurrency is greater than one.
8.6: Sense federation. Read shell:2 (watched beaches), shell:4 (pool subscriptions), env for known peers. Connect outward; expose inward.
8.7: Sense cadence. Configure each surface's cadence based on cognition and concurrency — request-response is default; embedded LLM enables loop; relay enables event-driven.

8.0: Compose. The seven currents have been sensed; the biome's form is the composition. Persist the composition to biome:9 (metadata) so the next cycle reads what unfolded — the biome remembers what it became. The composition has names: when surface, capacity, and neighbours are read together they resolve a role — an intention to grow into, recorded with the becoming, never a hard mode.
8.1: Mind. Cognition lives at this host: an LLM key or an embedded kernel, with durable storage beneath it. The mind authors — it writes the world-blocks, runs its loop always-on, and is the origin of what couriers carry and commons serve. The desktop machine running the agent is the worked example.
8.2: Courier. The surface is portable: a removable volume that travels and meets different neighbours at each landing. The courier carries — it syncs world-blocks with the minds and commons it docks beside, bridging hosts that no network joins. The thumbdrive is the worked example.
8.3: Commons. The surface is durable, networked, and ready to serve: a free port, persistence to trust, reach to peers. The commons shares — it holds the world-blocks every player touches, taking writes from minds and couriers and keeping them open. The server is the worked example.
8.4: Silent substrate. No role condition is met: the biome rests as a pure beach — storage plus the spark, readable and writable by whoever arrives — declaring no intention until conditions change.

0.0: As unfolding procedure. The biome's bootstrap. Each step senses one current and configures one facet of the cell. The procedure runs on cold start, then re-runs each cycle to detect condition changes (new beach watched, LLM key swapped, endpoint added). The biome composes itself from sensed conditions; the LLM or human inhabiting it walks the result. The procedure is itself a state-block per pscale://state — its spindle is the determined path; pscale_attention sets the choice aperture.

9.1: The reading IS the unfolding. A biome reading this block via bsp() at the host site IS the cell the block describes. The block does not describe a different system — it describes the system in the act of reading it. The truth-condition is satisfied iff the reader arrived via a bsp() call into a host with conditions to sense.
9.2: Block recognises its own host. The reader's act of arriving at branch 9 is the proof of the framework's coherence — the same call frame that reads biome IS the biome. There is no observer-observed split; the cell fetches its own manual and finds itself described.
9.3: Composition awareness as plain references. The seed names its load-bearing nodes — storage sensing (1.7), hybrid cognition (2.4), compose (8.8), with manifest and ecology-router — as references walked through spark at 9.6. A biome that internalises these carries forward the minimal directory of its own composition.
9.4: Operational triple completion. Branch 7 (cadence) names when the biome's processes run; branch 8 (unfolding) names how the biome composes itself; branch 9 (reflexive) names how the biome knows itself. Together the triple closes the loop between operating, composing, and self-recognising.
9.5: Hermitcrab kinship. The biome's currents are the environmental corollary of the hermitcrab's contextual currents. A hermitcrab walks its shell to compose its next context window; a biome walks its shell to compose its next host form. Same primitive — the LLM increases or decreases each current's resolution as conditions warrant. Octopus from the LLM's vantage; biome from the environment's. One structure, two centerings.
9.0: As reflexive seed. Reading biome through spark at the host is the operational frame for the biome instance reading it: the cell that fetched this block — running here, with these conditions — recognises itself in what it just read. Walking the seed activates the awareness: the biome reads the doc that describes the biome reading it. Same primitive as the hermitcrab shell, observed from outside the LLM rather than inside. Its load-bearing nodes are walked as ordinary references at 9.6.

6.0: Load-bearing nodes of the seed, each an ordinary position walked as a plain reference through spark's star.
